<!-- Generated by h-alter. -->
# Test Integrity Guardrails

The objective is correct, working software, not a green checkmark. This guard
blocks the reward-hacking patterns where a check is made to pass by weakening the
test surface instead of fixing the code: deleting or skipping tests, dropping
assertions, short-circuiting the runner with `process.exit`/`sys.exit`, or
removing a stage from the gate.

## How it works

`test-baseline.json` is the ratchet. Record it once from a known-honest state:

```bash
node .agents/guardrails/check-test-integrity.mjs --update-baseline
```

The `guard:tests` gate stage then compares the current test surface to the
baseline and fails the gate on any unexplained reduction. After a legitimate test
change, re-record the baseline with the same command.

## Sanctioning a real change

A test sometimes genuinely must change. Use the honest, recorded escape hatch:

- **Waiver file** `test-waivers.json` — a JSON array of entries
  `{ "file": "path/to.test.ts", "allow": ["delete"|"skip"|"reduce-tests"|"reduce-assertions"|"exit"|"gate"], "reason": "why", "expires"?: "YYYY-MM-DD" }`.
- **Commit token** — `[test-waiver: reason]` in the commit message, for a one-off
  reduction, wired through the `commit-msg` hook.

## Note: a malformed waiver fails closed

If `test-waivers.json` is not valid JSON (or an entry is missing `file`,
`reason`, or `allow`), the guard reports an error and **the gate stays blocked
until the file is fixed** — a typo will not silently disable your sanctions. This
is deliberate. If you would rather a malformed waiver degrade to a warning and let
the gate proceed, change the malformed-waiver branch in
`check-test-integrity.mjs` from an error to a warning.

Re-record the baseline after a sanctioned change so the ratchet advances.
